# How To Repair A Broken Whirlpool Dishwasher

![Dishwasher](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/images/product-dishwasher.svg)

Repairing a Whirlpool Dishwasher can seem like a daunting task, but 80% of Whirlpool Dishwasher repairs are rated by our customers as "Easy” and on average, take less than 15 minutes to complete. To help get you started, we’ve broken down Whirlpool Dishwasher repairs by the most common symptoms, like leaking or noisy. Click on the symptom your Whirlpool Dishwasher is experiencing and we'll show you how to troubleshoot the problem, what parts are at fault and how to get it repaired quickly and easily.

##  Common Broken Dishwasher Symptoms 

Reported By 

### [Noisy Learn how to troubleshoot and repair a noisy Whirlpool Dishwasher by the type of noise it makes, from the rattle of a pump housing or motor bushings, to a worn washer arm bearing. Reported By: 29% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Noisy/>) ### [Leaking Diagnose the reason for your leaking Whirlpool Dishwasher, from a worn pump and door seals, to a failed water inlet valve. Reported By: 27% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Leaking/>) ### [Will not start Learn how to fix a Whirlpool Dishwasher that will not start, by checking a few key components, such as door latch switches, timers, electronic controls, motor relays and thermal fuses. Reported By: 18% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Will-Not-Start/>) ### [Door latch failure Find out how to fix a Whirlpool Dishwasher door latch that isn't working properly by troubleshooting common repair parts like the door latch kit. Reported By: 11% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Door-Latch-Failure/>) ### [Not cleaning dishes properly Determine why your Whirlpool Dishwasher will not clean properly by troubleshooting parts, like housing gaskets, spray arms, water deliver tubes and filters. Reported By: 11% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Not-Cleaning-Properly/>) ### [Not draining Figure out why your Whirlpool Dishwasher will not drain by learning how to test a drain pump motor, timer and drain hose. Reported By: 9% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Not-Draining/>) ### [Will not fill with water Diagnose why your Whirlpool Dishwasher will not fill with water by checking and testing a water inlet valve, float switch and door switch. Reported By: 2% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Will-Not-Fill-Water/>) ### [Will not dispense detergent Learn how to repair a Whirlpool Dishwasher that will not dispense detergent by testing the bi-metal release, wax motor and timer. Reported By: 2% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Will-Not-Dispense-Detergent/>) ### [Not drying dishes properly Find out how to troubleshoot and repair a Whirlpool Dishwasher that will not properly dry dishes, by checking heating elements, high limit thermostats and rinse aid dispensers. Reported By: 1% of customers  ](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/Not-Drying-Properly/>)

## Troubleshooting Videos

![Troubleshooting Problems With Your Dishwasher Door Latch](https://img.youtube.com/vi/2Jy2G2FNSWg/maxresdefault.jpg)

[Troubleshooting Problems With Your Dishwasher Door Latch](<https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=2Jy2G2FNSWg>)

![Troubleshooting Why the Dishwasher Won't Clean Dishes](https://img.youtube.com/vi/-hs-vvHckBw/maxresdefault.jpg)

[Troubleshooting Why the Dishwasher Won't Clean Dishes](<https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=-hs-vvHckBw>)

![Troubleshooting Why the Dishwasher Won't Start](https://img.youtube.com/vi/8ZrtcdtmyEk/maxresdefault.jpg)

[Troubleshooting Why the Dishwasher Won't Start](<https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=8ZrtcdtmyEk>)

![Troubleshooting Why Your Dishwasher Is Leaking](https://img.youtube.com/vi/nbOp93Z5I8k/maxresdefault.jpg)

[Troubleshooting Why Your Dishwasher Is Leaking](<https://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player_embedded&v=nbOp93Z5I8k>)

structured_causes.cause: Wash Arm Bearing Ring & Spray Arm Seal
structured_causes.description: If your Whirlpool Dishwasher is noisy, the cause could be a wash arm seal or bearing ring. These parts are plastic rings that support the wash arm or the spray arm and that the arm rotates on. Either of these parts could be loose or be worn, causing the spray arm to not function properly. **How to inspect your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal:** 1. While you will not be disassembling your appliance, we still recommend unplugging your appliance before beginning.

Water is forced through the Whirlpool Dishwasher spray arms to spray water and detergent around the Whirlpool Dishwasher. All Whirlpool Dishwashers have a lower spray arm located at the bottom of the Whirlpool Dishwasher, and some Whirlpool Dishwashers also have middle or upper spray arms located below the top dish rack or at the top of the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub. **How to inspect Whirlpool Dishwasher spray arms:** 1. For safety, disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.

steps: Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this test; you will be handling electrical components.
Remove the pump in order to test it. The pump is usually found behind the lower access panel.

Using a multimeter on Rx1, place the probes onto the terminals to test for continuity. You should receive a reading of zero or nearly zero. To test the ground connection, with one probe still on a terminal, touch the other probe to the bare metal housing of the pump. You should not receive any reading from this test.
If your test results do not match those above, you will need a [replacement pump](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps").
While you will not be disassembling your appliance, we still recommend unplugging your appliance before beginning.
Locate your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal. They will be found attached to the spray/wash arms.
Visually inspect them for signs of wear, cracking, or damage. You may need to remove the spray/wash arms to fully inspect them, depending on your model.

If you find signs of the above symptoms, you will need [replacement wash arm bearing rings](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Bearings.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Bearings") and/or [spray arm seals.](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Seals")
For safety, disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
Manually spin your spray arms to ensure they are not hitting the racks and are turning freely. The spray arms should not be wobbly.
Visually inspect your spray arms for any signs of wear, cracking, or damage.
If you find any issues while performing the checks above, you will need [replacement spray arms](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Arms").

[REPAIR](</Repair/>) > [WHIRLPOOL DISHWASHER](</Repair/Dishwasher/Whirlpool/>) > NOISY 

# How To Fix A Noisy Whirlpool Dishwasher

![How To Fix A Noisy Whirlpool Dishwasher](https://img.youtube.com/vi/A8BeQ5qJyp4/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Pump ](<#Pump>) [ Wash Arm Bearing Ring & Spray Arm Seal ](<#WashArmBearing>) [ Spray Arms ](<#SprayArms>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as REALLY EASY
  * 989 repair stories
  * 10 step by step videos



## Pump

A defective pump could be the reason your Whirlpool Dishwasher is making loud noises. The function of the pump is to pressurize the spray arms, and in most models, it is also used to drain the water. The pump sits at the bottom of the Whirlpool Dishwasher and typically consists of two separate compartments; one for wash or circulation and the other for draining. The circulation part of the pump will typically consist of the wash impeller and the filter components while the drain portion will consist of a drain impeller and a chopper blade or in some cases, a solenoid operated diverter. The impellers are driven by an electric motor that is attached to the bottom or side of the pump.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher pump with a multimeter:**

  1. Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this test; you will be handling electrical components.
  2. Remove the pump in order to test it. The pump is usually found behind the lower access panel.
  3. Using a multimeter on Rx1, place the probes onto the terminals to test for continuity. You should receive a reading of zero or nearly zero. To test the ground connection, with one probe still on a terminal, touch the other probe to the bare metal housing of the pump. You should not receive any reading from this test.
  4. If your test results do not match those above, you will need a [replacement pump](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm>)

SEARCH

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Wash Arm Bearing Ring & Spray Arm Seal

If your Whirlpool Dishwasher is noisy, the cause could be a wash arm seal or bearing ring. These parts are plastic rings that support the wash arm or the spray arm and that the arm rotates on. Either of these parts could be loose or be worn, causing the spray arm to not function properly.

**How to inspect your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal:**

  1. While you will not be disassembling your appliance, we still recommend unplugging your appliance before beginning.
  2. Locate your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal. They will be found attached to the spray/wash arms.
  3. Visually inspect them for signs of wear, cracking, or damage. You may need to remove the spray/wash arms to fully inspect them, depending on your model.
  4. If you find signs of the above symptoms, you will need [replacement wash arm bearing rings](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Bearings.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Bearings") and/or [spray arm seals.](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Seals")

## Spray Arms

Water is forced through the Whirlpool Dishwasher spray arms to spray water and detergent around the Whirlpool Dishwasher. All Whirlpool Dishwashers have a lower spray arm located at the bottom of the Whirlpool Dishwasher, and some Whirlpool Dishwashers also have middle or upper spray arms located below the top dish rack or at the top of the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub.

**How to inspect Whirlpool Dishwasher spray arms:**

  1. For safety, disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
  2. Manually spin your spray arms to ensure they are not hitting the racks and are turning freely. The spray arms should not be wobbly.
  3. Visually inspect your spray arms for any signs of wear, cracking, or damage.
  4. If you find any issues while performing the checks above, you will need [replacement spray arms](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Arms").

# How To Repair A Leaking Whirlpool Dishwasher

![How To Repair A Leaking Whirlpool Dishwasher](https://img.youtube.com/vi/hBAfmgORE6Q/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Pump ](<#Pump>) [ Door Gasket ](<#DoorGasket>) [ Water Inlet Valve ](<#WaterInlet>) [ Dispensers & Grommets ](<#DispensersAnd>) [ Spray Arm ](<#SprayArm>) [ Float & Float Switch ](<#FloatAndFloat>) [ Hose Clamps & Hoses ](<#HoseClampsAnd>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as EASY
  * 1324 repair stories
  * 6 step by step videos



## Pump

A defective pump and/or pump seal may be the cause of a Whirlpool Dishwasher leak. The function of the pump is to circulate water in the Whirlpool Dishwasher and in some models, to drain the water as well. The pump typically consists of two separate compartments; one for wash or circulation and the other for draining. The circulation part of the pump will typically consist of the wash impeller and the filter components while the drain portion will consist of a drain impeller and a chopper blade or in some cases, a solenoid operated diverter.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher pump with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
  2. On most models, the pump will be found behind the lower access panel. Locate and remove your Whirlpool Dishwasher pump in order to test it.
  3. To test for continuity, set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch the terminals with the probes. This test should produce a reading of exactly or nearly zero. You should also check the ground connection of the pump. To do this, leave one probe on the terminal and touch the other to the bare metal housing of the pump. This test should not produce any reading.
  4. If your readings differ from the results above, you will need a [replacement pump](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm>)

## Door Gasket

If water appears to be leaking around the front of the Whirlpool Dishwasher, the problem may be with the door gasket. The Whirlpool Dishwasher door gasket or seal is normally made of soft rubber or vinyl and goes around the door or tub opening of the Whirlpool Dishwasher, creating a watertight seal. Some Whirlpool Dishwasher models have rubber baffles located at the bottom corners of the tub opening that prevent water leaking in that area. When vinyl or rubber ages, it can become less flexible or even brittle and may damage easily or not seal properly.

**How to inspect a door gasket on a Whirlpool Dishwasher:**

  1. Open your Whirlpool Dishwasher door to locate the door gasket, and baffles if applicable.
  2. Visually inspect the gasket for any signs of wearing, damage, missing pieces, or misalignment.
  3. If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement door gasket](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Seals").

## Water Inlet Valve

The water inlet valve feeds the water from your home’s main water line into the Whirlpool Dishwasher and could be the source of a leak. The inlet valve can be found behind the lower kick plate. The hot water inlet supply to the valve may be copper tubing, rubber hose or braided hose.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher water inlet valve:**

  1. Similar to most other inspections, begin by disconnecting your appliance from both the power and the water source.
  2. In order to access the water inlet valve, you will need to remove the lower access panel of your appliance. The valve will likely be located on the left side.
  3. Begin by inspecting the body of the valve. You are looking for any signs of cracking, damage, wear, or any other indication of a leak.
  4. If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement water inlet valve](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Valves.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Valves").

## Dispensers & Grommets

The detergent dispenser and the rinse aid dispenser are normally located on the inner door panel of the Whirlpool Dishwasher. Some models use a dispenser assembly that is attached to the panel with screws and a gasket to seal against water leaks. Other models may use a dispenser that is molded into the door panel but uses a dispenser latch assembly that is attached to the back of the inner panel and use a rubber grommet to seal around the dispenser latch where it protrudes through the door panel. If you have a water leak that appears to come from the front of your Whirlpool Dishwasher, the dispenser gasket or the dispenser latch grommet may be the cause.

**How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher detergent/rinse aid dispenser & grommets:**

Next, you will need to manually activate the relay by turning it upside down and allowing the plunger to drop. Once it is activated, touch the probes to the terminals of the relay. # How to Fix a Whirlpool Dishwasher That Won’t Start

![How to Fix a Whirlpool Dishwasher That Won’t Start](https://img.youtube.com/vi/XgZq_VdXKiQ/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Door Latch Or Door Latch Switch ](<#DoorLatchOr>) [ Timer Or Electronic Control ](<#TimerOrElectronic>) [ Selector Switch ](<#SelectorSwitch>) [ Motor Start Relay ](<#MotorStart>) [ Thermal Fuse ](<#ThermalFuse>) [ Drive Motor ](<#DriveMotor>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as EASY
  * 1817 repair stories
  * 7 step by step videos



## Door Latch Or Door Latch Switch

If your Whirlpool Dishwasher won’t start, the problem could be the door latch or door latch switches. The door latch assembly is used to hold the door closed during the cycle to prevent water from leaking and it also incorporates the door latch switches that supply power to the Whirlpool Dishwasher controls. If the door cannot close properly to activate the door latch switches or if the switches are defective, then the Whirlpool Dishwasher controls will not receive power and the Whirlpool Dishwasher will not start. Inspect the latch assembly including the switches.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher door latch assembly with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Begin by removing the inner door panel to gain access to the door latch assembly. It is usually found at the top of the door. Once you have located it, verify that the door catch activates the door latch switches by inserting the catch into the assembly.
  3. If the switches are being activated mechanically, remove the switches to check them for continuity. Using a multimeter on Rx1, touch the probes to the switch’s terminals. This should produce a reading of zero or nearly zero.
  4. If you receive a different reading, you will need a [replacement door latch assembly](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Latches.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Latches").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Latches](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Latches.htm>)

## Timer Or Electronic Control

If you have determined that the door latch assembly is operating properly and your Whirlpool Dishwasher still won’t start, the next step would be to look at the timer or electronic control. The first step of the Whirlpool Dishwasher cycle is normally a drain function to remove any standing water in the tub. If you do not hear the drain pump motor running, then you should look at the timer or electronic control as a possible cause. On manual timer models, the timer is used to supply power to the pump motor, water inlet valve, heater circuit and drain pump motor at the proper sequence in the cycle. The timer uses a series of electrical contacts that are driven by a small motor all of which are encased in the timer housing.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher timer with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source.
  2. Locate and remove the timer. It will most likely be found in the control panel, but it is also often found behind the lower kick plate.
  3. Some timers may have multiple sets of contacts/wires, if this is true for your model, you will need to refer to your wiring diagram to determine which contacts to test.
  4. Set your multimeter to Rx1000 and touch the contacts with the probes. Most functioning timers will fall in the range of 2000-3500 ohms of resistance, but this varies between models. Refer to your owner's manual to determine what reading your test should produce.
  5. If your test results differ from the manufacturer's recommendations, you will need a [replacement timer](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Timers.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Timers").

## Selector Switch

The selector switch on a Whirlpool Dishwasher is used to select different options for the individual cycles, such as heated dry and heated wash cycles. Depending on the model involved, the switch may be used in the motor or fill circuit and could cause a Whirlpool Dishwasher to not start if it were defective or if the selector buttons were not fully depressed. Check to ensure that the switch is properly depressed before attempting any disassembly. The contacts of the selector switch can be checked for continuity with a multimeter using the schematic diagram as a guide.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher selector switch with a multimeter:**

  1. Begin by disconnecting your appliance from the power source.
  2. Remove the inner door panel of your Whirlpool Dishwasher in order to locate the selector switch. It most likely will be found on the control panel. Once you have located it, ensure that the switch is properly depressed. If it is, remove it from the appliance in order to test it.
  3. Using a multimeter set to Rx1, touch the probes to the terminals of the switch, you will need to test each button individually. This should produce a reading of infinity. Next, with the probes still on the terminals, press in on the button of the terminals you are testing. This should change your reading to zero. Repeat this test for all other buttons.

# How To Fix A Broken Whirlpool Dishwasher Door Latch

![How To Fix A Broken Whirlpool Dishwasher Door Latch](https://img.youtube.com/vi/2Jy2G2FNSWg/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Door Latch Assembly ](<#DoorLatch>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as REALLY EASY
  * 1239 repair stories
  * 4 step by step videos



## Door Latch Assembly

If your door latch is not working properly, the first thing to inspect should be the door catch. The door catch is attached to the top of the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub and is normally adjustable. If not adjusted properly it can cause the door latch to not activate and will prevent the Whirlpool Dishwasher from working, or it may not close the door tight enough to prevent a leak. The door latch may also be worn and no longer creates a tight fit, in which case the door latch will need to be replaced.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher door latch assembly with a multimeter:**

  1. Verify that you have unplugged your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning.
  2. The door latch assembly is most often found inside the inner door panel, near the top of the door. Remove your inner door panel and locate the door latch assembly.
  3. Inspect the door catch to verify that it is mechanically activating the switches. If it is, remove the switches in order to test them for continuity.
  4. Set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch the terminals with the probes. You are testing for continuity and should receive a reading of exactly or nearly zero.
  5. If your test produces any other reading, you will need a [replacement door latch assembly](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Latches.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Latches").

## Spray Arm

The lower spray arm (as well as the upper and middle spray arms if applicable) could be the reason why your Whirlpool Dishwasher is not cleaning properly. The function of the spray arms is to spray the dishes with a strong stream of hot water and detergent. If some of the holes are plugged, then you should also inspect the filter system for a possible problem. Cracks or separated seams in the spray arms will impair the force of the spray as well. If your Whirlpool Dishwasher has an upper spray arm also inspect the spray arm mounting bracket to make sure it hasn't become loose, cracked or otherwise restricted.

**How to inspect Whirlpool Dishwasher spray arms:**

  1. Disconnect the appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Open your Whirlpool Dishwasher and visually inspect your spray arms. You are looking for any signs of wear, cracking, or damage. Inspect the holes of the arms to see if any dirt or debris is lodged in them, clean out anything you find.
  3. Turn the spray arms manually. While doing so, you should be inspecting if there is much resistance, if the arms are wobbling, or if the arms hit the dish racks.
  4. If your spray arms show any of the above symptoms, you will need [replacement spray arms](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Arms").

Disconnect your appliance from the power source.

All Whirlpool Dishwashers will have a method to drain the water. Most models will have a single motor driven pump with two separate compartments, one for circulation and one for drain, each with its own impeller. Other types will have a separate drain pump, and some will utilize the main circulating pump in conjunction with a drain solenoid and diverter valve or flapper. On models that use a separate drain pump, you should check to see if there is any obstruction to the input and output of the p

Some Whirlpool Dishwasher models will use a check valve in the drain outlet. The check valve is used to allow water to flow in one direction but will prevent the dirty water from re-entering the Whirlpool Dishwasher. If you suspect that the drain hose has a restriction or if you find that wastewater is getting back into the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub, then the check valve is most likely the problem. **How to inspect the check valve flapper in a Whirlpool Dishwasher:** 1. To ensure your safety, unp

Some older Whirlpool Dishwashers may use a belt driven pump. If the belt has come off or is slipping, then the pump that drains the Whirlpool Dishwasher won’t function properly. **How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher belt:** 1. Similar to other Whirlpool Dishwasher inspections, disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.

Some Whirlpool Dishwashers will use a mechanical timer to operate the cycles. The timer controls the main pump motor as well as the drain solenoid or separate drain pump motor if your model has that style. The timer is normally located in the control panel at the top of the Whirlpool Dishwasher door. You will require a wiring diagram and schematic to identify the correct timer contacts that control the drain cycle. These can then be checked for continuity with a multi-meter and if defective then

A Whirlpool Dishwasher will not drain properly if it has a restricted or clogged drain hose. Restrictions typically appear most often at the outlet from the pump or drain housing where a check valve may be located, at the input to the household drain system or anywhere that a kink may have formed in the drain hose. If food debris has caused a restriction, then you should check the condition of the food chopper as a possible source of the problem. **How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher drain hos

steps: Disconnect your appliance from the power source.
Locate your piston & nut assembly. It likely will be found on the bottom of the tub, beneath the sump cover.

Inspect your piston & nut assembly to determine if it is functioning properly. The assembly should move up and down freely. When the piston is in the down position, it should form a tight seal. If it is not forming a complete seal, the Whirlpool Dishwasher will not completely pump out the water.
If you find any issues while performing the inspection, you will need a [replacement piston & nut assembly](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").
Before beginning, ensure that you have disconnected your appliance from the power source.
Locate and remove your Whirlpool Dishwasher pump. It likely will be found behind the lower access panel.

Once you have removed it, using a multimeter on Rx1 mode, touch the pump’s terminals with the probes. You are testing for continuity and should receive a reading of zero or nearly zero. Next, you should test the ground connection by touching one of the probes to the bare metal housing while leaving one on a terminal. This test should not produce any reading.
If the results of your tests are different from the above, you will need a [replacement pump](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps").
To ensure your safety, unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this inspection.

Locate your check valve flapper in order to inspect it. In most models, it will be found on the outlet port of the drain pump or housing. You will need to remove the lower access panel in order to inspect it.
Visually inspect the flapper for any signs of wear or damage. You should also check to see if there is any dirt or debris preventing the flapper from moving freely, remove anything you may find.

If your valve is damaged, or if you find no objects preventing it from opening properly, you will need a [replacement check valve flapper](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").
Similar to other Whirlpool Dishwasher inspections, disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.

Your Whirlpool Dishwasher belt will be found behind the lower access panel, attached to a pulley on the pump assembly and to another pulley on the motor assembly. Remove the belt in order to closely inspect it.
Looking for any signs of burning, stretching, cracking, damage, or wear, inspect the belt.
If you find any issues while inspecting it, you will need a [replacement belt](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").
Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this test.
Your timer will likely be found either in the control panel or behind the lower kickplate. Locate and remove your appliance’s timer in order to test it.
If your model has multiple sets of contacts/wires, you will need to refer to your owner’s manual to determine which sets to test.

Remove the timer from your appliance, set your multimeter to Rx1000, and touch the contacts/wires with the probes. In most models, a functional timer will produce a reading of 2000-3500 ohms of resistance, but this varies from model to model. Please refer to your owner's manual to determine what reading you should see.

## Piston & Nut Assembly

Some models of Whirlpool Dishwashers utilize a check valve as part of the drain pump. Within the check valve, there is the piston and nut assembly. If your Whirlpool Dishwasher is not draining water properly, the piston and nut assembly could be the source of the problem.

**How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher piston & nut assembly:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source.
  2. Locate your piston & nut assembly. It likely will be found on the bottom of the tub, beneath the sump cover.
  3. Inspect your piston & nut assembly to determine if it is functioning properly. The assembly should move up and down freely. When the piston is in the down position, it should form a tight seal. If it is not forming a complete seal, the Whirlpool Dishwasher will not completely pump out the water.
  4. If you find any issues while performing the inspection, you will need a [replacement piston & nut assembly](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").

## Drain Pump & Motor

All Whirlpool Dishwashers will have a method to drain the water. Most models will have a single motor driven pump with two separate compartments, one for circulation and one for drain, each with its own impeller. Other types will have a separate drain pump, and some will utilize the main circulating pump in conjunction with a drain solenoid and diverter valve or flapper. On models that use a separate drain pump, you should check to see if there is any obstruction to the input and output of the pump and also verify that there is power getting to the pump motor during the drain portion of the cycle.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher pump with a multimeter:**

  1. Before beginning, ensure that you have disconnected your appliance from the power source.
  2. Locate and remove your Whirlpool Dishwasher pump. It likely will be found behind the lower access panel.
  3. Once you have removed it, using a multimeter on Rx1 mode, touch the pump’s terminals with the probes. You are testing for continuity and should receive a reading of zero or nearly zero. Next, you should test the ground connection by touching one of the probes to the bare metal housing while leaving one on a terminal. This test should not produce any reading.
  4. If the results of your tests are different from the above, you will need a [replacement pump](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Pumps](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm>)

## Check Valve Flapper

Some Whirlpool Dishwasher models will use a check valve in the drain outlet. The check valve is used to allow water to flow in one direction but will prevent the dirty water from re-entering the Whirlpool Dishwasher. If you suspect that the drain hose has a restriction or if you find that wastewater is getting back into the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub, then the check valve is most likely the problem.

**How to inspect the check valve flapper in a Whirlpool Dishwasher:**

  1. To ensure your safety, unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this inspection.
  2. Locate your check valve flapper in order to inspect it. In most models, it will be found on the outlet port of the drain pump or housing. You will need to remove the lower access panel in order to inspect it.
  3. Visually inspect the flapper for any signs of wear or damage. You should also check to see if there is any dirt or debris preventing the flapper from moving freely, remove anything you may find.
  4. If your valve is damaged, or if you find no objects preventing it from opening properly, you will need a [replacement check valve flapper](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").

## Belt

Some older Whirlpool Dishwashers may use a belt driven pump. If the belt has come off or is slipping, then the pump that drains the Whirlpool Dishwasher won’t function properly.

**How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher belt:**

  1. Similar to other Whirlpool Dishwasher inspections, disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Your Whirlpool Dishwasher belt will be found behind the lower access panel, attached to a pulley on the pump assembly and to another pulley on the motor assembly. Remove the belt in order to closely inspect it.
  3. Looking for any signs of burning, stretching, cracking, damage, or wear, inspect the belt.
  4. If you find any issues while inspecting it, you will need a [replacement belt](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").

## Timer

Some Whirlpool Dishwashers will use a mechanical timer to operate the cycles. The timer controls the main pump motor as well as the drain solenoid or separate drain pump motor if your model has that style. The timer is normally located in the control panel at the top of the Whirlpool Dishwasher door. You will require a wiring diagram and schematic to identify the correct timer contacts that control the drain cycle. These can then be checked for continuity with a multi-meter and if defective then the timer will need to be replaced.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher timer with a multimeter:**

  1. Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before beginning this test.
  2. Your timer will likely be found either in the control panel or behind the lower kickplate. Locate and remove your appliance’s timer in order to test it.
  3. If your model has multiple sets of contacts/wires, you will need to refer to your owner’s manual to determine which sets to test.

# How To Fix A Whirlpool Dishwasher That Will Not Fill With Water

![How To Fix A Whirlpool Dishwasher That Will Not Fill With Water](https://img.youtube.com/vi/9UxFcTXFewQ/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Water Inlet Valve ](<#WaterInlet>) [ Float ](<#Float>) [ Float Switch ](<#FloatSwitch>) [ Door Switch ](<#DoorSwitch>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as EASY
  * 368 repair stories
  * 3 step by step videos



## Water Inlet Valve

The water inlet valve is used to fill the Whirlpool Dishwasher with the proper amount of hot water and is controlled by the timer or electronic control. The valve will be located behind the lower access panel and will have two wires attached to it, an outlet hose to the tub and a hot water inlet from the household supply. If your Whirlpool Dishwasher does not fill, then you should first check the inlet supply to verify that you have enough water pressure.

**How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher water inlet valve:**

  1. Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher and disconnect the water supply before starting.
  2. Locate your water inlet valve. It is commonly found on the lower left side of your Whirlpool Dishwasher. You likely will need to remove the lower access panel to locate it.
  3. Looking for any signs of damage, wear, or cracking, visually inspect the water inlet valve.
  4. If your valve shows signs of any of the symptoms described above, you will need a [replacement water inlet valve](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Valves.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Valves").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Valves](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Valves.htm>)

Back to Top

## Float

A malfunctioning float could cause the Whirlpool Dishwasher to not fill with water. The acts as a safety device that prevents the Whirlpool Dishwasher from overfilling with water. The float rises with the water level in the Whirlpool Dishwasher and when the proper level is reached, the float will trigger the float switch to turn off the water inlet valve.

**How to check a Whirlpool Dishwasher float assembly:**

  1. Before beginning, verify that you have unplugged your appliance.
  2. Begin by opening your Whirlpool Dishwasher door and removing the cover over the float assembly. Once you’ve done that, manually lift the float and let it drop multiple times, it should move freely, if it does not, remove the float to remove any foreign objects in the guide tube. You should also inspect the float for any visible wear or damage.
  3. Remove the lower access panel to determine if the float is mechanically engaging with the float switch.
  4. If you find any issues while performing this inspection, you will need a [replacement float](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm>)

[How to check the float](</dishwasher+check-float-assembly+repair.htm>)[How to replace the float](</dishwasher+replace-float-assembly+repair.htm>)

Back to Top

## Float Switch

When the float switch is triggered by the float, power to the water inlet valve is interrupted and it stops the flow of water into the Whirlpool Dishwasher.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher float switch with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your Whirlpool Dishwasher from the power source before beginning this inspection; you will be handling electrical components.
  2. Locate and remove the float switch. It will be found behind the lower access panel, sitting directly below the float assembly. Use caution when disconnecting the wires from the switch, do not pull directly on the wires.
  3. Once you have removed it, test the float switch by setting your multimeter to Rx1 and touching the probes to the switch’s terminals. This should produce a reading of zero or infinity. With the probes still touching the terminals, press in on the switch’s button, this should change the reading to the opposite extreme (from zero to infinity or infinity to zero).
  4. If your test results produce readings that differ from the above, you will need a [replacement float switch](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Switches.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Switches").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Switches](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Switches.htm>)

Back to Top

## Door Switch

The door switch is a simple on/off mechanism that prohibits the Whirlpool Dishwasher from operating when the door is open. If the door switch or switches are malfunctioning the Whirlpool Dishwasher will not fill with water.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher door switch with a multimeter:**

  1. Before beginning, ensure that you unplug your appliance as you will be working with electrical components.
  2. The door switch is typically located behind the door panel at the top of the door. You will need to separate the inner door from the outer door in order to access the switch. Once you have located it, remove the door switch in order to test it for continuity.
  3. Using a multimeter set to Rx1, test for continuity by touching one probe to the COM terminal and the other probe to the N.O. Terminal. With the actuator NOT pushed in, your reading should be infinity. Next, keeping the probes on the terminals, press in on the actuator until you hear a click, this should change the reading to zero.
  4. If the results of your test do not match the results above, you will need a [replacement door switch](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Switches.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Switches").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Switches](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Switches.htm>)

Disconnect your appliance from the power source.

The rinse aid cap could be the reason why your Whirlpool Dishwasher is not releasing rinse aid. The rinse aid cap could be loose and may just need to be refitted, but could also be warped or damaged from the heat of the water in the Whirlpool Dishwasher. **How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher rinse aid cap:** 1. This is a very simple inspection; no tools or disassembly will be needed.

If your Whirlpool Dishwasher is not dispensing detergent, the spring and hinge pin on the dispenser door could be the problem. The dispenser door is spring loaded and is held in the closed position by a catch. **How to inspect the door spring & hinge pin in a Whirlpool Dishwasher:** 1. No tools or disassembly will be required; this is a very simple inspection.

The wax motor is used in newer model Whirlpool Dishwashers to release the detergent dispenser door catch. If the wax motor is not working properly, then the dispenser door will not open to release the detergent. The wax motor acts as a solenoid when activated by the Whirlpool Dishwasher’s timer or control, and the piston operates the mechanism that will release the catch for the dispenser door. **How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher wax motor with a multimeter:** 1. Disconnect your appliance from

Some older model Whirlpool Dishwashers use a bi-metal release to operate the detergent dispenser door catch. The bi-metal release uses electric current to create a mechanical action to release the door catch. **How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher bi-metal release with a multimeter:** 1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning this or any other test.

Some models of Whirlpool Dishwashers will use the timer to mechanically operate the detergent dispenser. At the proper time in the cycle, the timer will activate a lever that releases the detergent door catch. Check that the linkage between the timer and the door catch is working properly and if not replace it. Verify that the cam on the timer is working properly and replace the timer if defective. **How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher timer with a multimeter:** 1. Begin by disconnecting the powe

steps: Disconnect your appliance from the power source.
Locate your dispenser, you will not need to remove it to inspect it. It will most likely be found attached to the inside of the Whirlpool Dishwasher door.
Visually inspect the dispenser for any signs of cracking, discoloration, or damage. If you find any caked-on detergent or debris on the dispenser, thoroughly clean it with a wet cloth.
If you find any of the above issues, you will need a [replacement dispenser](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Dispensers.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Dispensers").
This is a very simple inspection; no tools or disassembly will be needed.
The rinse aid dispensing cap is located on the inner door panel of your Whirlpool Dishwasher. Remove the cap from the door in order to inspect it.
Looking for any signs of melting, warping, cracking, or discoloration, visually inspect the rinse aid cap.
If you find any issues while performing the inspection above, you will need a [replacement rinse aid cap](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Caps-and-Lids.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Caps").
No tools or disassembly will be required; this is a very simple inspection.

The door spring & hinge pin will be found on the inside of the Whirlpool Dishwasher door, on some models it may be covered by the detergent dispenser assembly. Remove any components covering it if applicable.
Visually inspect the spring and pin to see if they are bent, broken, corroded, or otherwise damaged.

If you find any issues with your spring and pin, you will need a [replacement door spring & hinge pin](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Springs-and-Shock-Absorbers.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Springs").
Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.

Depending on your model, you will need to remove the external door panel or the internal door panel in order to access the wax motor, which will be located near the detergent dispenser cup. Remove the wax motor in order to test it.
Using a multimeter on the Rx1 setting, touch the probes to one terminal each to test it. If you receive a reading of infinity, the motor is defective.
If your motor fails the test above, you will need a [replacement wax motor](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Motors.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Motors").
Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning this or any other test.

Locate the bi-metal release. Depending on your model, you will need to remove either the internal or the external door panel in order to access the bi-metal release. Before removing the release, with the panel removed you should check the alignment of the release. You can do this by closing the detergent cup and pushing in on the metal with a small screwdriver until the detergent cup opens. Often only a slight adjustment is needed to solve this issue.

If this did not fix your issue, remove the release, set your multimeter to Rx1, and touch the probes to the release’s terminals. If your multimeter shows a reading of infinity, your release is defective.
If your test results indicate your release is faulty, you will need a [replacement bi-metal release](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").

## Door Spring & Hinge Pin

If your Whirlpool Dishwasher is not dispensing detergent, the spring and hinge pin on the dispenser door could be the problem. The dispenser door is spring loaded and is held in the closed position by a catch.

**How to inspect the door spring & hinge pin in a Whirlpool Dishwasher:**

  1. No tools or disassembly will be required; this is a very simple inspection.
  2. The door spring & hinge pin will be found on the inside of the Whirlpool Dishwasher door, on some models it may be covered by the detergent dispenser assembly. Remove any components covering it if applicable.
  3. Visually inspect the spring and pin to see if they are bent, broken, corroded, or otherwise damaged.
  4. If you find any issues with your spring and pin, you will need a [replacement door spring & hinge pin](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Springs-and-Shock-Absorbers.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Springs").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Springs and Shock Absorbers](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Springs-and-Shock-Absorbers.htm>)

SEARCH

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Wax Motor

The wax motor is used in newer model Whirlpool Dishwashers to release the detergent dispenser door catch. If the wax motor is not working properly, then the dispenser door will not open to release the detergent. The wax motor acts as a solenoid when activated by the Whirlpool Dishwasher’s timer or control, and the piston operates the mechanism that will release the catch for the dispenser door.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher wax motor with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Depending on your model, you will need to remove the external door panel or the internal door panel in order to access the wax motor, which will be located near the detergent dispenser cup. Remove the wax motor in order to test it.
  3. Using a multimeter on the Rx1 setting, touch the probes to one terminal each to test it. If you receive a reading of infinity, the motor is defective.
  4. If your motor fails the test above, you will need a [replacement wax motor](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Motors.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Motors").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Motors](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Motors.htm>)

Back to Top

## Bi-Metal Release

Some older model Whirlpool Dishwashers use a bi-metal release to operate the detergent dispenser door catch. The bi-metal release uses electric current to create a mechanical action to release the door catch.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher bi-metal release with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning this or any other test.
  2. Locate the bi-metal release. Depending on your model, you will need to remove either the internal or the external door panel in order to access the bi-metal release. Before removing the release, with the panel removed you should check the alignment of the release. You can do this by closing the detergent cup and pushing in on the metal with a small screwdriver until the detergent cup opens. Often only a slight adjustment is needed to solve this issue.
  3. If this did not fix your issue, remove the release, set your multimeter to Rx1, and touch the probes to the release’s terminals. If your multimeter shows a reading of infinity, your release is defective.
  4. If your test results indicate your release is faulty, you will need a [replacement bi-metal release](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts").



Start Your Repair Here [View All Whirlpool Dishwasher Parts](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Parts.htm>)

## Heating Element

A malfunctioning heating element may be the cause for dishes not drying. To test the heating element, first, make sure that power to your Whirlpool Dishwasher is turned off, and then check for continuity with a multi-meter.

**How to test the heating element in a Whirlpool Dishwasher with a multimeter:**

  1. Unplug your Whirlpool Dishwasher before attempting this test.
  2. Your heating element will be found inside the Whirlpool Dishwasher, attached to the bottom of the tub. It may be covered or uncovered. If it is covered, remove the cover.
  3. In order to disconnect the heating element, you will need to remove the lower access panel. Remove the wires attached to the heating element’s terminals by carefully grasping the metal connector, do not pull on the wire itself.
  4. To test the element, set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch each probe to a terminal. Your reading should be somewhere between zero and infinity, refer to your owner’s manual to determine what reading you should receive for your model. If it is exactly zero or infinity it is defective.
  5. If your test produces a reading different from the manufacturer’s recommendation, you will need a [replacement heating element](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Elements-and-Burners.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Element").

## High Limit Thermostat

The high limit thermostat acts as a safety device that keeps the Whirlpool Dishwasher from getting too hot. If the thermostat is malfunctioning, it could shut off the heat before your dishes are finished drying.

**How to test a Whirlpool Dishwasher high limit thermostat with a multimeter:**

  1. Ensure that you unplug your appliance before beginning this inspection.
  2. Locate and remove the thermostat in order to test it for continuity. In most models, it can be found at the bottom of the Whirlpool Dishwasher tub, behind the lower access panel.
  3. Set your multimeter to Rx1 mode and touch the terminals with the probes. When your thermostat is at room temperature, you should receive a reading of infinity. If you place the thermostat next to a warm lamp, you should receive a reading of zero.
  4. If your thermostat produces different results, you will need a [replacement high limit thermostat](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Thermostats.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Thermostats").

**How to inspect a Whirlpool Dishwasher rinse aid dispenser:**

  1. This inspection is very simple; you will not need to disassemble your appliance or have any tools to complete this inspection.
  2. Your rinse aid dispenser will be found on the inside of the door, remove the cap from the dispenser.
  3. After verifying that there is rinse aid in the chamber, inspect the cap and chamber for any signs of discoloration, warping, or any other damage.
  4. If you find any issues with your rinse aid dispenser while performing this inspection, you will need a [replacement rinse aid dispenser](</Whirlpool-Dishwasher-Dispensers.htm> "OEM Replacement Whirlpool Dishwasher Dispensers").

# How To Fix A Noisy GE Dishwasher

![How To Fix A Noisy GE Dishwasher](https://img.youtube.com/vi/A8BeQ5qJyp4/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Pump ](<#Pump>) [ Wash Arm Bearing Ring & Spray Arm Seal ](<#WashArmBearing>) [ Spray Arms ](<#SprayArms>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as REALLY EASY
  * 989 repair stories
  * 10 step by step videos



## Pump

A defective pump could be the reason your GE Dishwasher is making loud noises. The function of the pump is to pressurize the spray arms, and in most models, it is also used to drain the water. The pump sits at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher and typically consists of two separate compartments; one for wash or circulation and the other for draining. The circulation part of the pump will typically consist of the wash impeller and the filter components while the drain portion will consist of a drain impeller and a chopper blade or in some cases, a solenoid operated diverter. The impellers are driven by an electric motor that is attached to the bottom or side of the pump.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher pump with a multimeter:**

  1. Unplug your GE Dishwasher before beginning this test; you will be handling electrical components.
  2. Remove the pump in order to test it. The pump is usually found behind the lower access panel.
  3. Using a multimeter on Rx1, place the probes onto the terminals to test for continuity. You should receive a reading of zero or nearly zero. To test the ground connection, with one probe still on a terminal, touch the other probe to the bare metal housing of the pump. You should not receive any reading from this test.
  4. If your test results do not match those above, you will need a [replacement pump](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Pumps").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Pumps](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm>)

![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/2353893_1_N.jpg) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/259582_1_N.jpg) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/260804_1_N.jpg)

SEARCH

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Wash Arm Bearing Ring & Spray Arm Seal

If your GE Dishwasher is noisy, the cause could be a wash arm seal or bearing ring. These parts are plastic rings that support the wash arm or the spray arm and that the arm rotates on. Either of these parts could be loose or be worn, causing the spray arm to not function properly.

**How to inspect your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal:**

  1. While you will not be disassembling your appliance, we still recommend unplugging your appliance before beginning.
  2. Locate your wash arm bearing ring & spray arm seal. They will be found attached to the spray/wash arms.
  3. Visually inspect them for signs of wear, cracking, or damage. You may need to remove the spray/wash arms to fully inspect them, depending on your model.
  4. If you find signs of the above symptoms, you will need [replacement wash arm bearing rings](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Bearings.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Bearings") and/or [spray arm seals.](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Seals")



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Bearings](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Bearings.htm>)

![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/343923_1_N.jpg) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/402231_2_N.JPG) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/402231_1_N.JPG)

SEARCH

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Spray Arms

Water is forced through the GE Dishwasher spray arms to spray water and detergent around the GE Dishwasher. All GE Dishwashers have a lower spray arm located at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher, and some GE Dishwashers also have middle or upper spray arms located below the top dish rack or at the top of the GE Dishwasher tub.

**How to inspect GE Dishwasher spray arms:**

  1. For safety, disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
  2. Manually spin your spray arms to ensure they are not hitting the racks and are turning freely. The spray arms should not be wobbly.
  3. Visually inspect your spray arms for any signs of wear, cracking, or damage.
  4. If you find any issues while performing the checks above, you will need [replacement spray arms](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Arms").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Spray Arms](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm>)

structured_causes.description: A defective pump and/or pump seal may be the cause of a GE Dishwasher leak. The function of the pump is to circulate water in the GE Dishwasher and in some models, to drain the water as well. The pump typically consists of two separate compartments; one for wash or circulation and the other for draining. The circulation part of the pump will typically consist of the wash impeller and the filter components while the drain portion will consist of a drain impeller and a chopper blade or in some cas

If water appears to be leaking around the front of the GE Dishwasher, the problem may be with the door gasket. The GE Dishwasher door gasket or seal is normally made of soft rubber or vinyl and goes around the door or tub opening of the GE Dishwasher, creating a watertight seal. Some GE Dishwasher models have rubber baffles located at the bottom corners of the tub opening that prevent water leaking in that area. When vinyl or rubber ages, it can become less flexible or even brittle and may damag

The water inlet valve feeds the water from your home’s main water line into the GE Dishwasher and could be the source of a leak. The inlet valve can be found behind the lower kick plate. The hot water inlet supply to the valve may be copper tubing, rubber hose or braided hose. **How to test a GE Dishwasher water inlet valve:** 1. Similar to most other inspections, begin by disconnecting your appliance from both the power and the water source.

The detergent dispenser and the rinse aid dispenser are normally located on the inner door panel of the GE Dishwasher. Some models use a dispenser assembly that is attached to the panel with screws and a gasket to seal against water leaks. Other models may use a dispenser that is molded into the door panel but uses a dispenser latch assembly that is attached to the back of the inner panel and use a rubber grommet to seal around the dispenser latch where it protrudes through the door panel. If yo

Water is forced through the GE Dishwasher spray arms to spray water and detergent around the GE Dishwasher. All GE Dishwashers have a lower spray arm located at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher, and some GE Dishwashers also have middle or upper spray arms located below the top dish rack or at the top of the GE Dishwasher tub. Most spray arms are made of plastic which can crack or warp enough to change the spray pattern significantly. Metal spray arms can become damaged or separate at the seams wh

The float and float switch are used as a safety device to prevent the GE Dishwasher from overfilling with water. The float is located inside of the tub at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher and the float switch is located beneath the tub directly below the float. The float lifts as the water level in the GE Dishwasher rises, and when the proper water level is reached, the stem of the float activates the float switch to turn off the water inlet valve. A malfunctioning float or float switch could cau

The hose clamps secure the drain hose and the circulating hose to the GE Dishwasher pump. These hoses and clamps can be found by removing the kickplate at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher. If the clamps have come loose or the hoses have cracks in them, it could be the cause of a leak. Be sure to check the clamps on both the drain hose and the circulating hose. **How to inspect GE Dishwasher hoses:** 1. Disconnect the water supply as well as the power source from your appliance before beginning.

steps: Disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
On most models, the pump will be found behind the lower access panel. Locate and remove your GE Dishwasher pump in order to test it.

To test for continuity, set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch the terminals with the probes. This test should produce a reading of exactly or nearly zero. You should also check the ground connection of the pump. To do this, leave one probe on the terminal and touch the other to the bare metal housing of the pump. This test should not produce any reading.
If your readings differ from the results above, you will need a [replacement pump](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Pumps").
Open your GE Dishwasher door to locate the door gasket, and baffles if applicable.
Visually inspect the gasket for any signs of wearing, damage, missing pieces, or misalignment.
If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement door gasket](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Seals").
Similar to most other inspections, begin by disconnecting your appliance from both the power and the water source.
In order to access the water inlet valve, you will need to remove the lower access panel of your appliance. The valve will likely be located on the left side.
Begin by inspecting the body of the valve. You are looking for any signs of cracking, damage, wear, or any other indication of a leak.
If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement water inlet valve](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Valves.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Valves").
For safety, unplug your GE Dishwasher before beginning.
Open the GE Dishwasher door to visually inspect the dispenser.

First, inspect the lever for cracking or damage, a broken lever would prevent the dispenser from opening or closing properly. Next, inspect the dispenser cover for any wear or damage. Finally, inspect the container itself for cracks or other damage.
If you find any issues while performing the inspections above, you will need a [replacement dispenser](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Dispensers.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Dispenser").
While not crucial, we recommend you unplug your GE Dishwasher before starting this inspection.
Begin by inspecting the arms for any signs of cracking, wear, or any other damage.

Next, manually spin the spray arms. You should be feeling to see if there is any resistance, if they are wobbling, or if they hit the dish racks. Additionally, inspect to see if any of the holes in the arms are clogged, clear out anything you may find.
If you find any of the issues described above, you will need [replacement spray arms](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Arms").
Disconnect your GE Dishwasher from the power source as well as the water supply.

# How To Repair A Leaking GE Dishwasher

![How To Repair A Leaking GE Dishwasher](https://img.youtube.com/vi/hBAfmgORE6Q/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

[ Pump ](<#Pump>) [ Door Gasket ](<#DoorGasket>) [ Water Inlet Valve ](<#WaterInlet>) [ Dispensers & Grommets ](<#DispensersAnd>) [ Spray Arm ](<#SprayArm>) [ Float & Float Switch ](<#FloatAndFloat>) [ Hose Clamps & Hoses ](<#HoseClampsAnd>)

### About this repair:

  * Rated as EASY
  * 1324 repair stories
  * 6 step by step videos



## Pump

A defective pump and/or pump seal may be the cause of a GE Dishwasher leak. The function of the pump is to circulate water in the GE Dishwasher and in some models, to drain the water as well. The pump typically consists of two separate compartments; one for wash or circulation and the other for draining. The circulation part of the pump will typically consist of the wash impeller and the filter components while the drain portion will consist of a drain impeller and a chopper blade or in some cases, a solenoid operated diverter.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher pump with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect the power from your appliance before beginning.
  2. On most models, the pump will be found behind the lower access panel. Locate and remove your GE Dishwasher pump in order to test it.
  3. To test for continuity, set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch the terminals with the probes. This test should produce a reading of exactly or nearly zero. You should also check the ground connection of the pump. To do this, leave one probe on the terminal and touch the other to the bare metal housing of the pump. This test should not produce any reading.
  4. If your readings differ from the results above, you will need a [replacement pump](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Pumps").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Pumps](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Pumps.htm>)

![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/1990918_1_N.jpg) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/1765174_1_N.jpg) ![](https://partselectcom-gtcdcddbene3cpes.z01.azurefd.net/assets/partimages/2340929_1_N.jpg)

SEARCH

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Door Gasket

If water appears to be leaking around the front of the GE Dishwasher, the problem may be with the door gasket. The GE Dishwasher door gasket or seal is normally made of soft rubber or vinyl and goes around the door or tub opening of the GE Dishwasher, creating a watertight seal. Some GE Dishwasher models have rubber baffles located at the bottom corners of the tub opening that prevent water leaking in that area. When vinyl or rubber ages, it can become less flexible or even brittle and may damage easily or not seal properly.

**How to inspect a door gasket on a GE Dishwasher:**

  1. Open your GE Dishwasher door to locate the door gasket, and baffles if applicable.
  2. Visually inspect the gasket for any signs of wearing, damage, missing pieces, or misalignment.
  3. If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement door gasket](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Seals").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Seals and Gaskets](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm>)

[Need help finding your model number?](</Find-Your-Model-Number/>)

Back to Top

## Water Inlet Valve

The water inlet valve feeds the water from your home’s main water line into the GE Dishwasher and could be the source of a leak. The inlet valve can be found behind the lower kick plate. The hot water inlet supply to the valve may be copper tubing, rubber hose or braided hose.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher water inlet valve:**

  1. Similar to most other inspections, begin by disconnecting your appliance from both the power and the water source.
  2. In order to access the water inlet valve, you will need to remove the lower access panel of your appliance. The valve will likely be located on the left side.
  3. Begin by inspecting the body of the valve. You are looking for any signs of cracking, damage, wear, or any other indication of a leak.
  4. If you find any of the above, you will need a [replacement water inlet valve](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Valves.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Valves").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Valves](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Valves.htm>)

## Dispensers & Grommets

The detergent dispenser and the rinse aid dispenser are normally located on the inner door panel of the GE Dishwasher. Some models use a dispenser assembly that is attached to the panel with screws and a gasket to seal against water leaks. Other models may use a dispenser that is molded into the door panel but uses a dispenser latch assembly that is attached to the back of the inner panel and use a rubber grommet to seal around the dispenser latch where it protrudes through the door panel. If you have a water leak that appears to come from the front of your GE Dishwasher, the dispenser gasket or the dispenser latch grommet may be the cause.

**How to inspect a GE Dishwasher detergent/rinse aid dispenser & grommets:**

  1. For safety, unplug your GE Dishwasher before beginning.
  2. Open the GE Dishwasher door to visually inspect the dispenser.
  3. First, inspect the lever for cracking or damage, a broken lever would prevent the dispenser from opening or closing properly. Next, inspect the dispenser cover for any wear or damage. Finally, inspect the container itself for cracks or other damage.
  4. If you find any issues while performing the inspections above, you will need a [replacement dispenser](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Dispensers.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Dispenser").

## Spray Arm

Water is forced through the GE Dishwasher spray arms to spray water and detergent around the GE Dishwasher. All GE Dishwashers have a lower spray arm located at the bottom of the GE Dishwasher, and some GE Dishwashers also have middle or upper spray arms located below the top dish rack or at the top of the GE Dishwasher tub. Most spray arms are made of plastic which can crack or warp enough to change the spray pattern significantly. Metal spray arms can become damaged or separate at the seams which can also alter the spray pattern. These conditions can cause water to be directed at the bottom of the door where there is no gasket and therefore creating a water leak.

# How to Fix a GE Dishwasher That Won’t Start

![How to Fix a GE Dishwasher That Won’t Start](https://img.youtube.com/vi/XgZq_VdXKiQ/maxresdefault.jpg)

### Click a Part Below to Start Your Repair:

### About this repair:

  * Rated as EASY
  * 1817 repair stories
  * 7 step by step videos



## Door Latch Or Door Latch Switch

If your GE Dishwasher won’t start, the problem could be the door latch or door latch switches. The door latch assembly is used to hold the door closed during the cycle to prevent water from leaking and it also incorporates the door latch switches that supply power to the GE Dishwasher controls. If the door cannot close properly to activate the door latch switches or if the switches are defective, then the GE Dishwasher controls will not receive power and the GE Dishwasher will not start. Inspect the latch assembly including the switches.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher door latch assembly with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Begin by removing the inner door panel to gain access to the door latch assembly. It is usually found at the top of the door. Once you have located it, verify that the door catch activates the door latch switches by inserting the catch into the assembly.
  3. If the switches are being activated mechanically, remove the switches to check them for continuity. Using a multimeter on Rx1, touch the probes to the switch’s terminals. This should produce a reading of zero or nearly zero.
  4. If you receive a different reading, you will need a [replacement door latch assembly](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Latches.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Latches").



Start Your Repair Here [View All General Electric Dishwasher Latches](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Latches.htm>)

## Timer Or Electronic Control

If you have determined that the door latch assembly is operating properly and your GE Dishwasher still won’t start, the next step would be to look at the timer or electronic control. The first step of the GE Dishwasher cycle is normally a drain function to remove any standing water in the tub. If you do not hear the drain pump motor running, then you should look at the timer or electronic control as a possible cause. On manual timer models, the timer is used to supply power to the pump motor, water inlet valve, heater circuit and drain pump motor at the proper sequence in the cycle. The timer uses a series of electrical contacts that are driven by a small motor all of which are encased in the timer housing.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher timer with a multimeter:**

  1. Disconnect your appliance from the power source.
  2. Locate and remove the timer. It will most likely be found in the control panel, but it is also often found behind the lower kick plate.
  3. Some timers may have multiple sets of contacts/wires, if this is true for your model, you will need to refer to your wiring diagram to determine which contacts to test.
  4. Set your multimeter to Rx1000 and touch the contacts with the probes. Most functioning timers will fall in the range of 2000-3500 ohms of resistance, but this varies between models. Refer to your owner's manual to determine what reading your test should produce.
  5. If your test results differ from the manufacturer's recommendations, you will need a [replacement timer](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Timers.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Timers").

## Selector Switch

The selector switch on a GE Dishwasher is used to select different options for the individual cycles, such as heated dry and heated wash cycles. Depending on the model involved, the switch may be used in the motor or fill circuit and could cause a GE Dishwasher to not start if it were defective or if the selector buttons were not fully depressed. Check to ensure that the switch is properly depressed before attempting any disassembly. The contacts of the selector switch can be checked for continuity with a multimeter using the schematic diagram as a guide.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher selector switch with a multimeter:**

  1. Begin by disconnecting your appliance from the power source.
  2. Remove the inner door panel of your GE Dishwasher in order to locate the selector switch. It most likely will be found on the control panel. Once you have located it, ensure that the switch is properly depressed. If it is, remove it from the appliance in order to test it.
  3. Using a multimeter set to Rx1, touch the probes to the terminals of the switch, you will need to test each button individually. This should produce a reading of infinity. Next, with the probes still on the terminals, press in on the button of the terminals you are testing. This should change your reading to zero. Repeat this test for all other buttons.
  4. If any of your readings do not match the results above, you will need a [replacement selector switch](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Switches.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Switches").

# How To Fix A Broken GE Dishwasher Door Latch

### About this repair:

  * Rated as REALLY EASY
  * 1239 repair stories

## Door Latch Assembly

If your door latch is not working properly, the first thing to inspect should be the door catch. The door catch is attached to the top of the GE Dishwasher tub and is normally adjustable. If not adjusted properly it can cause the door latch to not activate and will prevent the GE Dishwasher from working, or it may not close the door tight enough to prevent a leak. The door latch may also be worn and no longer creates a tight fit, in which case the door latch will need to be replaced.

**How to test a GE Dishwasher door latch assembly with a multimeter:**

  1. Verify that you have unplugged your GE Dishwasher before beginning.
  2. The door latch assembly is most often found inside the inner door panel, near the top of the door. Remove your inner door panel and locate the door latch assembly.
  3. Inspect the door catch to verify that it is mechanically activating the switches. If it is, remove the switches in order to test them for continuity.
  4. Set your multimeter to Rx1 and touch the terminals with the probes. You are testing for continuity and should receive a reading of exactly or nearly zero.
  5. If your test produces any other reading, you will need a [replacement door latch assembly](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Latches.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Latches").

## Upper Discharge Housing Gasket

If you are experiencing low pressure from the spray arms, the upper discharge housing gasket could be the source of the problem.

**How to inspect a GE Dishwasher upper discharge housing gasket:**

  1. For safety, unplug your GE Dishwasher before starting this repair.
  2. This housing gasket is located on the discharge cover above the circulation impeller. The gasket may have just come loose, or it may need to be replaced altogether. Locate your gasket in order to inspect it.
  3. Inspect the gasket for any signs of wear, damage, cracking, missing chunks, and if it is properly attached. If it is loose, ensure that there is no debris that is preventing a proper seal and then reattach it.
  4. If you find your housing gasket is damaged or worn, you will need a [replacement upper discharge housing gasket](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Seals-and-Gaskets.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Gaskets").

## Spray Arm

The lower spray arm (as well as the upper and middle spray arms if applicable) could be the reason why your GE Dishwasher is not cleaning properly. The function of the spray arms is to spray the dishes with a strong stream of hot water and detergent. If some of the holes are plugged, then you should also inspect the filter system for a possible problem. Cracks or separated seams in the spray arms will impair the force of the spray as well. If your GE Dishwasher has an upper spray arm also inspect the spray arm mounting bracket to make sure it hasn't become loose, cracked or otherwise restricted.

**How to inspect GE Dishwasher spray arms:**

  1. Disconnect the appliance from the power source before beginning.
  2. Open your GE Dishwasher and visually inspect your spray arms. You are looking for any signs of wear, cracking, or damage. Inspect the holes of the arms to see if any dirt or debris is lodged in them, clean out anything you find.
  3. Turn the spray arms manually. While doing so, you should be inspecting if there is much resistance, if the arms are wobbling, or if the arms hit the dish racks.
  4. If your spray arms show any of the above symptoms, you will need [replacement spray arms](</General-Electric-Dishwasher-Spray-Arms.htm> "OEM Replacement GE Dishwasher Arms").
